primary_diagnosis.name: Anaphylaxis (food-triggered, peanut) with upper-airway oedema and distributive shock

differential.evidence_basis: Rapid multi-system reaction (skin+airway+GI) after a known allergen.
Wheeze and breathlessness — but does not explain urticaria/angioedema/stridor.
Skin/mucosal only — excluded once airway and circulatory involvement present.
Cannot explain objective hypotension, hives or facial swelling.
ASCIA criteria met: known allergen + rapid skin + airway + circulatory involvement.
Stridor and angioedema are not features of pure lower-airway asthma.
Confirmed constellation; no effective adrenaline yet delivered — time-critical.

red_flags.description: Inspiratory stridor / tongue and throat swelling — impending upper-airway obstruction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Airway is the immediate threat to life; can progress to complete occlusion within minutes.

red_flags.description: Hypotension / lightheadedness / impending doom — distributive (anaphylactic) shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: Circulatory collapse; adrenaline and supine positioning are time-critical.
The single definitive treatment has not been given — every minute of delay increases mortality.

red_flags.description: Rapid multi-system progression within 15 minutes of a known allergen
red_flags.clinical_significance: Speed of onset predicts severity; biphasic reaction risk mandates hospital observation.

pathophysiology_summary: IgE-mediated mast-cell and basophil degranulation on re-exposure to peanut allergen releases histamine, tryptase and other mediators, producing capillary leak (angioedema, urticaria, hypotension), bronchospasm and upper-airway oedema. Untreated, distributive shock and airway occlusion cause death. Intramuscular adrenaline reverses the mediator effects (α1 vasoconstriction, β2 bronchodilation, mast-cell stabilisation) and is the only first-line treatment; antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts that do NOT treat the airway or shock.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the wheeze as asthma and reaching for salbutamol instead of adrenaline.
Giving an oral antihistamine as the primary treatment — it does nothing for airway or circulation and delays adrenaline.
Being reassured by a transient improvement and under-triaging — biphasic reactions recur hours later.
Telehealth trap: attempting to manage at home rather than escalating; the definitive drug (adrenaline) plus airway backup requires the autoinjector now and an ambulance.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure and heart rate
Auscultated air entry / degree of bronchospasm
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance + instruct use of any in-date adrenaline autoinjector into the outer thigh + supine/seated positioning; repeat adrenaline after 5 minutes if no improvement.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (constellation is classic); the real risk is UNDER-action — managing at home instead of escalating, or substituting an antihistamine/inhaler for adrenaline.
